Your pasted summary is mostly accurate, but it mixes together three different layers of GitHub project management that are worth separating:


1. Issues are the work records
GitHub uses issues as the base work item for bugs, features, tasks, and larger initiatives. Issues can use templates/forms, assignees, labels, sub-issues, task lists, and dependencies to structure work. Labels and milestones are mainly attached at the issue/PR level, not owned by Projects itself. GitHub Docs+1


2. Projects is the planning surface
A project is an adaptable collection of items that can be shown as a table, board, or roadmap. It stays synced with GitHub data and can track issues, pull requests, and draft ideas. Projects also support filtering, sorting, grouping, multiple saved views, and team-specific metadata through fields. GitHub Docs+1


3. Fields, automation, and charts are what make Projects useful
Projects lets you add custom fields such as text, number, date, single-select, and iteration. GitHub’s quickstart specifically shows using fields for things like priority, estimates, and iterations. Projects also includes built-in workflows that can set status automatically, including default workflows that mark items Done when issues/PRs close or PRs merge. GitHub also supports charts/insights built from project items and filters. GitHub Docs+3GitHub Docs+3GitHub Docs+3


A more precise way to think about GitHub project management is:


Repository = where code and docs live

Issue / PR = the actual unit of work

Labels / milestones / assignees / dependencies = work metadata and coordination

Project = the cross-item planning system

Views / fields / automation / charts = the management layer on top of the work items GitHub Docs+1


A few useful corrections and additions to your summary:


Roadmaps are not a separate product. They are a project view that uses date or iteration fields to place items on a timeline. GitHub also supports milestone markers in roadmap view. GitHub Docs

Draft issues matter more than your summary suggests. Projects can track ideas before they become repo issues, then later convert them into issues. That is one of the cleaner native planning features. GitHub Docs+1

Project templates are real native functionality at the organization level, so standardizing views, fields, and workflows is supported natively. GitHub Docs+1

The integrations part of your pasted text is not wrong in broad terms, but it is not the core of GitHub project management. Native GitHub PM is already built around Projects, Issues, PRs, automations, and insights. Third-party apps are optional extensions, not the center of the model. GitHub Marketplace does have a project-management category for apps. GitHub+1


For your use case, the main takeaway is this:


GitHub Projects is not a replacement for issues. It is a synchronization and visualization layer over issues, pull requests, and draft planning items. The actual project-management power comes from combining:


issue structure,

project fields,

saved views,

built-in automation,

and roadmap/chart reporting. GitHub Docs+2GitHub Docs+2

GitHub continues to expand its project‑management features beyond the core capabilities you looked at earlier. Recent docs and changelog notes highlight a few important additions, and the open‑source community has released several new command‑line tools and actions to make Projects V2 easier to manage.


New/updated developer documentation (2025‑2026)


Issue fields (public preview, 2026). GitHub is introducing organization‑wide issue fields that carry typed metadata such as Priority, Effort, Start date and Target date across every repository. These fields replace unstructured labels; they are defined once at the org level and can be added as columns in private Projects to group, filter and chart items. Four field types are available (single‑select, text, number and date), and up to 25 fields per organization are supportedgithub.blog. Issue fields are editable directly in project views, sync back to the underlying issue, support search/filtering, are included in charts, and the API/webhooks expose field_added and field_removed eventsdocs.github.com. The feature is currently in public previewdocs.github.com.

Iteration fields. Iteration fields allow teams to define repeating time blocks (sprints) and group items accordingly. You can filter with keywords like @current, @previous and @next and use comparison operators (>, <, ..)docs.github.com. When an iteration field is created, three iterations are automatically generated; you can add more, insert breaks, edit names/dates and bulk‑move items between iterationsdocs.github.com. The roadmap view uses iteration fields to show work on a timelinedocs.github.com.

Import items with search queries (Feb 2026). Projects can now be initialized with a search query instead of selecting specific repositories. The query supports the same issue/PR filters available on the Issues page (AND/OR, nested queries) and helps create projects with exactly the desired itemsgithub.blog.

Hierarchy view general availability (Mar 2026). The hierarchy view for Projects, which shows parent–child relationships (sub‑issues), is now on by default. Improvements include easier sub‑issue filters and automatic application of existing filters to newly added sub‑issuesgithub.blog.

Hierarchy view improvements and file‑upload fields (Mar 2026). Filtering sub‑issues (using syntax such as sub‑issues.is:open), deduplicating duplicate items, and persisting expand/collapse state have been addedgithub.blog. Issue forms now support a new upload field type so reporters can attach logs/screenshots when opening issuesgithub.blog. Projects can also bulk‑move all items from one iteration to another from the group headergithub.blog.

Agent sessions (Mar 2026). When a coding agent (Copilot, Claude, etc.) is assigned to an issue, a live session indicator now appears in the issue sidebar and in project table/board views. The session displays statuses like “queued”, “working” or “waiting for review” and lets users jump into logsgithub.blog.

GitHub MCP Server updates (Oct 2025). The GitHub MCP server (which powers natural‑language agents) added a Projects toolset that can list projects, retrieve items/fields, update field values, and add or remove issues/PRsgithub.blog. It is not enabled by default; administrators must add it to the server configurationgithub.blog.


Additional open‑source tools & GitHub Actions

Tool/Repository
Key capabilities
gh‑pm (CLI extension)raw.githubusercontent.com
A comprehensive command‑line extension for GitHub Projects V2. Supports listing/creating/updating issues and fields, bulk triage, issue intake, project‑selection caching, and smart mapping of status/priority. The quick‑start includes commands for creating issues with priority, moving items between statuses, splitting issues into sub‑issues and running triage in dry‑run moderaw.githubusercontent.com.
gh‑projects (CLI extension)raw.githubusercontent.com
Fills a gap until the official GitHub CLI supports Projects V2. Provides commands to clone, edit, list and view projects. For example, it can clone a project with a new title, edit fields and issues, or list projects filtered by search termsraw.githubusercontent.com.
gh‑migrate‑project (CLI extension)raw.githubusercontent.com
Migrates Projects between GitHub.com and Enterprise instances. Supports moves from Enterprise Server to GitHub.com, between orgs or accounts, and to/from Enterprise Cloud with data residency. The README outlines steps to install, export a project with a PAT (read:project and repo scopes), map repositories, and import to the destinationraw.githubusercontent.com.
gh‑sub‑issue (CLI extension)raw.githubusercontent.com
Manages sub‑issue relationships. Lets you link existing issues as sub‑issues, create new sub‑issues with labels/assignees, list sub‑issues, and remove links. Supports multiple output formats and cross‑repository linkingraw.githubusercontent.com.
update‑project‑fields GitHub Actiongithub.com
Similar to update-project-v2-item-field but uses a PAT with repo and project scopes (not org‑wide). After adding an item via add-to-project, this action accepts an item-id plus comma‑separated field names and values to update text, number, date, single‑select or iteration fieldsgithub.com.
gh‑project‑automation tools (previously noted)
Continue using community actions like project‑beta‑automations (auto‑add and move items, manage custom fields), github‑project‑automation‑plus (move/create project cards based on events), update‑project‑v2‑item‑field (set status or iteration fields from actions) and the official actions/add‑to‑project (add issues/PRs to a project with label filters).
GitHub Project Manager (MCP server & CLI)mcpmarket.com
A third‑party tool marketed via MCP Market. Provides a CLI and MCP server for end‑to‑end management of Projects V2: environment setup (statuses, views, fields, labels, workflows), daily issue/PR management, backlog management, sprint analytics (velocity reports), and migration from Jira, Linear or Notionmcpmarket.com.
GitHub MCP Servergithub.blog
Official server that now includes a projects toolset for AI agents. Agents can list projects, retrieve items/fields, update item statuses, and add/remove issues or PRsgithub.blog.

How to use these resources


Adopt Issue & Iteration fields. If your org is eligible for the issue‑fields preview, start by pinning priority/effort fields to relevant issue types. Use iteration fields to define consistent sprint lengths, then group project views by iteration to see work distributiondocs.github.com.

Leverage changelog improvements. Turn on hierarchy view and agent session displays in project views. Use the new “import by query” feature when spinning up a project to avoid manual filteringgithub.blog. In issue forms, consider adding an upload field to collect logs or screenshots at submission timegithub.blog.

Choose the right CLI/action. For heavy command‑line workflows, install gh‑pm for a full suite of project management commands. Use gh‑projects for simple clone/edit/list tasks, gh‑sub‑issue to manage hierarchical issues, and gh‑migrate‑project when moving projects across accounts. In GitHub Actions, combine add‑to‑project with update‑project‑fields or update‑project‑v2‑item‑field to populate and update fields automaticallygithub.com.

Explore MCP/AI integrations. If you’re building agentic workflows, configure the GitHub MCP server to include the projects toolset and/or the GitHub Project Manager skill. These enable agents to list projects, update item statuses, and generate sprint reports through natural language requestsgithub.blog.


These resources should help you stay up‑to‑date with the latest GitHub Projects features and give you a toolkit of actions and CLI extensions for advanced automation.

intake → classification → planning → execution routing → status sync → evidence capture → reporting → escalation

The main question is not whether full autonomy is possible. It is. The real question is where to let GitHub stay declarative and where to move logic into your own orchestrator.


Bottom line


For a fully autonomous end-to-end process, the strongest architecture is:


GitHub Issues = source work objects

GitHub Projects = state machine and planning surface

GitHub Actions = event bus and enforcement layer

GraphQL/API + CLI tools = mutation engine

MCP/AI = reasoning layer, summarization layer, and assisted decision layer

Your own orchestrator = deterministic policy engine for anything beyond GitHub’s native workflows


That is the durable model.


1. Adopt Issue fields and Iteration fields as control surfaces, not just metadata


GitHub’s new issue fields are not just nicer labels. They are organization-level, typed metadata that can be pinned to issue types, searched, filtered, charted, and used in projects. They are currently in public preview, available only for select organizations, and only supported in private projects right now. GitHub says every eligible org gets four preconfigured fields: Priority, Effort, Start date, and Target date. They also expose full REST and GraphQL API support and field-related webhook events.


That changes the autonomy model in a major way:


Old model: labels carry weak, messy signals.

New model: issue fields become typed planning inputs.

Result: automation becomes less brittle.


A fully autonomous setup should treat issue fields as governing inputs:


Priority

Effort

Target date

Start date

optional custom org fields like Risk, Workstream, Automation class, Approval tier


Then treat project fields as execution-state outputs:


Status

Iteration

Phase

Completion

Agent session visible

Escalation state


GitHub docs confirm issue fields can be added as project columns and then used for edit, group, filter, sort, and charts. They also confirm a project’s total field count is capped at 50, and issue fields plus system fields count toward that limit.


Your fully autonomous design should therefore split fields into two classes:


Planning inputs


mostly set by humans, templates, import jobs, or AI intake agents


Execution outputs


mostly mutated automatically by Actions, CLI, or orchestrator logic


That avoids field chaos.


Recommended autonomous pattern


When an issue is created:


issue template or form captures structured intake

issue type determines which pinned fields appear

required field set is validated

item is auto-added to project

project item is assigned default Status, Phase, and Iteration

missing required values trigger automated comment / label / failure state


GitHub now supports searching issues by field values like field.priority:high and date comparisons like field."target date":>=2026-03-01, which means your automations can use these fields as routing primitives, not just display columns.


Iteration fields should be treated as autonomous scheduling rails


Iteration fields already support:


repeating time blocks

breaks

custom durations

filtering with @current, @next, @previous

roadmap usage

bulk move between iterations from grouped views


That means a deterministic automation loop can do this:


intake lands in backlog

policy engine maps item into an iteration based on priority, effort, due date, dependency state

end-of-iteration automation rolls unfinished work forward

charts and roadmaps update automatically

sprint carryover is measurable


GitHub now explicitly supports bulk-moving all items from one iteration to another from the group header, and the docs describe iteration configuration and roadmap integration.


What this means for full autonomy:
Iteration is no longer just a board view convenience. It can be the scheduling boundary that your automation system uses for planning and rollover.


2. Changelog improvements materially improve autonomous planning


Several recent GitHub Projects changes matter a lot for autonomy.


Hierarchy view


Hierarchy view is now generally available and enabled by default for new project views. Existing views can enable it with Show hierarchy. GitHub also added sub-issue filter improvements and auto-application of filters to sub-issues.


Earlier in 2026 GitHub also added:


inline creation of sub-issues from a project

drag-and-drop reorder/reparent

sync of sub-issue ordering between issues and projects

deduping and saved expand/collapse state improvements over subsequent releases


For autonomy, that matters because hierarchy lets you model:


initiative
phase
task
subtask


without losing project-board visibility.


A strong autonomous system should use this hierarchy to distinguish:


planning unit: parent issue

execution unit: child issue

atomic unit: sub-issue or linked task


That gives the orchestrator finer routing. Simple work can be done directly. Complex work can be decomposed.


Import by query


GitHub can now create a project and import items using a search query, not just a repo import. The query supports the same filters as the Issues page, including AND, OR, and nested queries.


That is a big autonomy lever.


Instead of manually populating new projects, you can have a bootstrap flow like:


create project from template

import all open issues matching:
is:open label:feature field.priority:high

milestone:"Q2" type:feature

label:phase-3 OR label:phase-4


That makes project provisioning scriptable.


Agent sessions in Issues and Projects


GitHub now shows coding-agent sessions under assignees in Issues and can surface them directly in project table and board views. Statuses include queued, working, waiting for review, and completed.


That matters because it closes a visibility gap:


Before:


AI work happened off to the side.


Now:


AI work can become a first-class project signal.


In a fully autonomous model, that enables:


“human owns issue, agent owns execution session”

project shows whether an item is actively being worked by an agent

stalled agent work becomes detectable

review queues become visible


This is the beginning of native AI-work telemetry inside GitHub planning.


Issue-form upload field and issue-template improvements


GitHub added an upload field type for issue forms, useful for logs, screenshots, crash reports, and evidence capture. GitHub also added Copilot assignment support in issue templates.


For autonomy, that means issue intake can become evidence-aware:


bug intake requires logs

UI issues require screenshots

deployment incidents require report bundle

failed automation runs can link back to structured evidence


That is exactly the kind of structured intake needed for end-to-end automation.


3. CLI/actions: where autonomy becomes real


This is where the system stops being “project management” and becomes an operational machine.


Native GitHub Action baseline


GitHub’s own docs say:


built-in workflows can automate status updates, auto-add, and auto-archive

GitHub Actions can automate project additions and field changes

GraphQL is the main API surface for Project V2 automation

GITHUB_TOKEN cannot access Projects for these workflows the way repository automation can; you typically need a PAT or GitHub App depending on use case


GitHub also maintains actions/add-to-project, which automatically adds the current issue or PR to a specified project, supports label filters, and is Project V2 only.


That gives you the baseline native automation chain:


event occurs

item is added to project

follow-up action or script mutates fields

built-in workflows keep status/archive behavior aligned


Your uploaded workflows already approximate this model


Your Project Item Sync workflow listens to issue and pull-request lifecycle events and passes field IDs, option IDs, label maps, and a project token into a Python sync script. That is exactly the right shape for deterministic state mutation.


TEMP_project_item_sync


Your Milestone Completion Sync workflow reacts to close/reopen events and updates status/completion fields. That gives you milestone-aware rollup automation.


TEMP_milestone_completion


Your Sync SPLINTER Phase Plan to GitHub Projects workflow watches phases/**/*.yml changes and syncs them into GitHub Projects. That is the clearest sign in your current files that you are trying to bridge external planning artifacts into GitHub’s work-management layer.


TEMP_splinter_phase_sync


Your Remote Sync Handler and self-hosted remote-to-local trigger files show a second automation rail: GitHub events causing local automation, with loop-prevention via sync: commit prefixes.


TEMP_remote-sync


TEMP_triggerA-remote-to-local


That means your current direction is already moving toward:


GitHub as state layer + external orchestrator as execution layer


That is the correct direction.


Best tool split


gh-pm


Best when you want daily operational control from CLI:


create/list/view/move project issues

intake untracked issues

triage in bulk

split into sub-issues

cache metadata and map common status/priority values


This is the strongest operator CLI in your current tool list because it is not just “project listing.” It is trying to be a practical PM control surface.


gh-projects


Best for lighter project-level operations:


clone

edit

list

view Project V2 boards from CLI


Useful, but not enough by itself for full autonomy.


gh-sub-issue


Best for deterministic decomposition:


add or create sub-issues

list/remove sub-issues

cross-repo support


This is strategically important because decomposition is one of the hardest parts of autonomous execution.


gh-migrate-project


Best for migration and template rollouts across orgs/accounts/environments.


Not daily-ops critical, but very useful if you want to stamp standardized project operating models across tenants.


update-project-v2-item-field / update-project-fields


These are useful when native built-in workflows are not enough and you need deterministic field mutation from Actions. They are best as small mutation utilities, not your orchestration brain.


What a fully autonomous Actions stack should do


A serious end-to-end flow should look like this:


Intake


issue opened from form

validate required fields/evidence

assign issue type

add to project

assign default status/phase/iteration

comment on failures


Planning sync


parse external phase/plan YAML

create/update parent issues

create/update sub-issues

bind fields and milestones

preserve idempotency keys


Execution routing


if task is documentation → docs pipeline

if task is code change → coding agent pipeline

if task is release/deploy → staging/prod pipeline

if task violates guardrails → block and escalate


Status mutation


PR ready_for_review → In Review

PR converted_to_draft → In Progress

issue blocked by dependency → Blocked

all child items done → parent moves toward done

milestone complete → completion field + status update


Evidence and quality


attach artifact links

update issue comment with summary

upload logs

create incident issue on failure

move item to escalation state


Rollup/reporting


weekly charts

sprint carryover

automation failure dashboards

stale-item scans

velocity / throughput / blocked count

The gap is not tooling. The gap is unified state design.


4. MCP/AI integrations: where reasoning belongs


GitHub’s MCP server added Project support in late 2025, including listing projects, retrieving project issues/PRs/fields, updating item fields, and adding/removing items. GitHub says the projects toolset is not enabled by default and must be added to configuration.


That means MCP is now viable for:


natural-language querying of project state

AI status summarization

AI triage assistance

guided updates to fields/items


But you should not put hard governance solely in MCP.


Correct role for MCP


MCP is strongest for:


interpretation

summarization

recommendation

operator convenience

conversational control of projects


MCP is weaker for:


deterministic enforcement

idempotent large-scale mutation

high-trust lifecycle orchestration

fail-closed release logic


So the right model is:


MCP = reasoning plane
Actions/orchestrator = enforcement plane


That split matters.


Strong agentic pattern


Use MCP or GitHub Project Manager skill for:


“show all blocked items in current iteration”

“summarize sprint risk”

“find stale items with no PR activity”

“recommend next iteration candidates”

“generate status update draft”


Do not use MCP alone for:


production deployments

authoritative field mutation chains

governance-critical merges

rollback logic

safety gates


Those belong in Actions + scripts + auditable state.


5. What “fully autonomous end-to-end” should mean here


There are three levels.


Level 1: Assisted automation


Humans still decide most things.
Automation only updates state.


GitHub native features are already very good here.


Level 2: Policy-driven autonomy


Humans define rules.
System executes routine decisions automatically.


This is where your current workflow files are heading. It is the sweet spot.


Level 3: Agentic autonomy


AI decomposes, routes, mutates, reports, and escalates with minimal human action.


Possible, but only safe if wrapped in:


hard state rules

allowed mutation boundaries

reversible operations

evidence capture

approval checkpoints


That means your system should not ask:
“Can AI manage the project?”


It should ask:
“Which transitions are safe to automate with no approval, which require review, and which require human signoff?”


6. Recommended target architecture for you


Given your existing files and your interest in zero-touch flows, I would structure it like this.


A. GitHub becomes the operational SSOT for work state


Not the only SSOT overall, but the operational SSOT for:


work items

project status

iteration

milestone completion

approvals

agent session visibility


B. External structured files remain SSOT for plan definitions


Your phase YAML and other planning artifacts should define:


canonical phases

decomposition rules

mappings

orchestration contracts


Then sync those into GitHub, the way your SPLINTER phase sync workflow already suggests.


TEMP_splinter_phase_sync


C. One orchestrator, not many half-orchestrators


Your uploaded workflows show a lot of useful single-purpose automations. That is fine at first, but long-term they should feed one master policy model, not drift independently.


Your scheduled orchestrator file is the seed of that idea. It runs daily, uploads reports, and can healthcheck.


TEMP_scheduled-orchestrator


That should evolve into the central controller for:


scheduled reconciliation

drift correction

rollup metrics

stuck-item detection

backlog hygiene

missing-field enforcement

phase sync reconciliation


D. GitHub Actions should stay thin


Actions should mostly:


receive event

validate preconditions

call orchestrator or focused script

write results

upload evidence


Do not let workflow YAML become the business logic layer.


7. The biggest risks in a fully autonomous GitHub PM system


Risk 1: state duplication


If labels, issue fields, project fields, milestones, and external YAML all represent the same thing, the system will drift.


Fix:


declare one source for each concept


Risk 2: automation collisions


Built-in workflows, third-party actions, CLI tools, and scripts can all mutate the same field.


Fix:


assign field ownership

document which actor owns each transition


Risk 3: idempotency failure


Repeated triggers can create duplicate items, comments, field flips, or PR noise.


Fix:


idempotency keys

mutation guards

compare-before-write


Risk 4: token/permission fragmentation


GitHub Projects automation often needs PAT or App permissions beyond repo-scoped defaults. GitHub explicitly documents the need for project or read:project scopes or an installation token depending on use case.


Fix:

LAW-003 — PROVABLE_PARALLEL_INDEPENDENCE


Parallel execution is forbidden unless independence is proven from declared scope.

declared writes do not overlap

exclusive resources do not overlap

no global state writer conflict exists

no undeclared side effects are present

Below is an updated formal mapping/enforcement layer that extends the prior design with five requested components: a reconciliation loop specification, an override policy, drift classification, convergent mutation rules, and checksum/version comparison rules. These additions make the plan→GitHub projection resilient to divergence and allow manual overrides and drift to be managed systematically.



1. Reconciliation loop specification


1.1 Architecture


The reconciliation engine continuously keeps GitHub (execution surface) in sync with the plan package (single source of truth). It runs a periodic loop and reacts to selected GitHub events.


plan package (desired state)
    ↓ validate
projection manifest (spec)
    ↓ apply → GitHub state (observed)
    ↓ compare → diff report
    ↓ act → corrective actions
    ↓ record → reconciliation report


1.2 Trigger sources


Scheduled: at least daily (or configurable in reconciliation_policy.interval).

Event-driven: on workflow run completion, pull request merge/close, issue creation/close, field change, plan update.

Manual: user request via CLI or API.


1.3 Loop stages


Discover current GitHub state (issues, project items, fields, milestones, PRs).

Compare desired state (projection manifest) and observed state, generating a structured diff by ID.

Classify each diff instance into a drift class (see Section 4).

Decide for each diff instance:
If plan‑owned, propose correction (plan → GitHub).

If GitHub‑owned, treat as acceptable or log.

If override by human, follow override policy (see Section 3).

Mutate: apply corrective actions via GraphQL/API in convergent fashion (Section 5).

Record: log actions and outcomes, update reconciliation records, and optionally comment on issues/PRs.

Update: recompute checksums/versions (Section 6) and store the new projection manifest.


1.4 Reporting


The reconciliation engine writes:


reconciliation_report.json summarizing diffs, actions, and drift classes.

drift_report.json with per‑item drift details.

Updates to github_projection_manifest.json with new checksums.



2. Override policy


Manual changes in GitHub are inevitable; the override policy formalises when they can override the plan package and how to handle them.


2.1 Allowed manual fields


GitHub‑owned fields: assignee, reviewers, conversation threads, labels unrelated to lifecycle or evidence.

Free‑text fields: descriptions and comments.

Evidence artefacts: attachments, logs, screenshots.


These are always allowed and will not trigger reconciliation correction, though they will be logged.


2.2 Plan‑owned fields


Fields governed by the plan (Phase, Status, EvidenceState, DeterminismState, PhasePlanID, StepID, WorkstreamID, ProgramSpecID, etc.) should not be manually changed in GitHub. Manual edits will be classified as high‑severity drift (see Section 4) and automatically reverted unless a formal override is issued.


2.3 Formal override mechanism


If a human wants to override the plan package for plan‑owned data:


They must submit a structured override request (via CLI or workflow) referencing the relevant ID and justification.

The override will be recorded in override_registry.json and will adjust the plan state.

Overrides may be temporary (pending plan update) or permanent (requiring new plan revision).


2.4 Emergency changes


In emergency contexts (e.g., high‑severity bug fix), a designated maintainer may apply an override directly to GitHub. The reconciliation engine will detect the change, classify it as override, and require immediate plan update or explicit override approval within 24 hours; otherwise it will revert.



3. Drift classification


Drift is a divergence between desired and observed state. Each drift instance is classified to determine the appropriate action.

Drift class
Description
Action
Class 0 (No drift)
Observed matches desired.
No action.
Class 1 (Allowed)
Legitimate human changes to GitHub‑owned fields (assignee, conversation, evidence attachments).
Log only.
Class 2 (Overridable)
Human edits to plan‑owned fields but filed through override mechanism or fall under emergency policy.
Mark as override and require plan update; optionally accept override for limited period.
Class 3 (Violation)
Unauthorized changes to plan‑owned fields (phase, status, step identity, etc.) or unlinked issues/PRs.
Revert changes, log violation, possibly open remediation issue.
Class 4 (Orphan)
GitHub items (issues, PRs, milestones) with no corresponding plan ID.
Convert into new plan entry or quarantine and notify planner.
Class 5 (Missing)
Plan items with no corresponding GitHub artefact.
Create missing artefact and log.

Drift classes should be logged in the reconciliation report. Class 3 and 5 are high severity.



4. Convergent mutation rules


To ensure idempotency and avoid oscillation, all state mutations must converge to the same final state regardless of ordering or repetition.


Upsert by identity: mutations are keyed by stable plan IDs. If an item exists, update fields; otherwise create.

Prefer immutability: once a stable ID (phase_plan_id, step_id) is bound to a GitHub item, it is never reused. Deleting and recreating an item must allocate a new ID.

No destructive overrides: if a field is plan‑owned and a drift exists, the default action is to update GitHub to match the plan. Only the override mechanism can change the plan.

Partial merges: when merging changes from plan to GitHub, update only plan‑owned fields; preserve GitHub‑owned fields untouched.

Conflict resolution: in case of conflicting changes (e.g., plan changed and human changed), reconciliation should respect override policy; if no override exists, plan wins.

Fail closed: if applying a mutation would lead to an invalid state (e.g., missing required ID), abort and raise an error.



5. Checksum/version comparison rules


5.1 Plan package checksum


Compute a content hash (e.g., SHA‑256) over the normalized plan JSON and projection manifest.

Store the hash in plan_manifest.json and include it in all projection metadata.


5.2 GitHub state versioning


Each GitHub artefact (issue, project item, field) should store a custom field (e.g., PlanStateVersion) containing the current plan package hash and an incrementing version number.

On projection apply, update the version and hash field. If the incoming hash matches the stored value, no update is needed.


5.3 Drift detection using checksums


During reconciliation, recompute the plan hash and compare it with the stored hash in GitHub. If they differ, classify the difference as drift and schedule corrections.

Use version comparison to prevent race conditions: only apply updates if the observed version equals the expected one; otherwise re‑fetch and recompute.


5.4 Rollback and replay


If a reconciliation cycle fails in the middle of updates, the version/checksum comparison rules allow safe retry. Unapplied patches will be re‑attempted until the state converges.

Keep a rolling history of at least the last five plan versions in plan_version_registry.json for audit and manual rollback if necessary.



6. Implementation artefacts and workflow integration


6.1 New required files


config/override_registry.json

schemas/reconciliation_report.schema.json

schemas/drift_report.schema.json

schemas/override_request.schema.json

scripts/reconcile_plan_and_github.py

scripts/apply_override.py

scripts/compute_plan_checksum.py

scripts/validate_drift_classes.py


6.2 New workflows


.github/workflows/reconciliation-loop.yml – runs scheduled and event‑driven reconciliation with reporting.

.github/workflows/apply-override.yml – processes override requests, updates plan and projection manifest.

.github/workflows/compute-checksum.yml – computes plan package hashes and updates GitHub state.



7. Responsibilities

Component
Responsibilities
Projection compiler
Generate initial GitHub artefacts from a validated plan; compute checksums; produce github_projection_manifest.json.
Reconciliation engine
Run the reconciliation loop; detect drift; classify drift; apply convergent mutations; update checksums and versions; handle overrides; report.
Override handler
Receive override requests; validate; update plan and projection manifest; trigger reconciliation.
Phase advancement evaluator
Evaluate composite phase exit criteria; update phase field.
Field bootstrap
Discover field and option IDs; maintain stable github_field_registry.json.


8. Summary


This revised layer positions the phase‑plan package as the single source of truth and treats GitHub as a projection and operational tracking surface. A deterministic projection compiler translates plan artefacts into GitHub issues, project items, fields and milestones. A reconciliation loop regularly compares the desired state to the observed state, classifies drift, respects manual overrides, applies convergent updates, and maintains checksums/versions for safe replays. Drift classification, override policy, convergent mutation rules, and checksum comparison ensure the system is predictable, auditable, and resilient.

Why this is mandatory


GitHub’s own documentation states plainly that GITHUB_TOKEN is repository-scoped and cannot access Projects; to access projects you must use either a GitHub App (recommended for organization projects) or a personal access token (recommended for user projects). citeturn2view0


Your assessment flags PAT reliance as a production liability at the scale you’re targeting (autonomous phase transitions, CI-triggered field updates, multi-repo workflows), and recommends GitHub App auth as the correct replacement. fileciteturn4file6


Formal rule


LAW-GH-001 — APP-FIRST AUTHENTICATION
All automation that reads/writes Projects must authenticate via a GitHub App installation token. PATs may exist only as an explicitly declared exception path.

App privilege requirements


GitHub’s Projects automation guide includes a critical note: you must grant the App read/write permission to organization projects; repository projects permission is not sufficient. citeturn2view2


Minimum permissions should be selected (“least privilege”) per GitHub’s guidance on GitHub App permissions. citeturn6view2

Use an installation token per workflow run (ephemeral credential):


Use actions/create-github-app-token to generate the installation token inside GitHub Actions. citeturn2view2turn0search0

The action generates a token via the /app/installations/{installation_id}/access_tokens endpoint. citeturn5search1turn5search5

Installation tokens expire about one hour after creation by design, so the system must generate new tokens rather than attempting token reuse. citeturn6view1

Convergent mutation rules


Govern all mutations by convergence principles supported by GitHub’s API semantics:


Upsert behavior
“ensure item exists” uses addProjectV2ItemById; if it already exists, GitHub returns the existing item ID (idempotent). citeturn1view3

Two-step mutation
GitHub requires separate calls to add an item vs update field values; do not attempt to combine. citeturn1view3

Field constraints
updateProjectV2ItemFieldValue can’t change assignees/labels/milestone/repository because those belong to issues/PRs, so governance must treat those as separate mutation channels. citeturn1view3

Backoff and budgets
must obey GraphQL and REST rate limits (primary and secondary) with backoff and concurrency ceilings. citeturn6view0turn6view3


Checksum and version comparison rules


Add two deterministic fields to GitHub Project items (project custom fields, not issue labels):


PlanHash (sha256 of canonical plan package / projection manifest)

ProjectionVersion (monotonic integer or semantic version)


Reconciliation compares:


desired PlanHash and ProjectionVersion (from plan package)
against

observed values in GitHub fields


If mismatch:


classify drift

apply convergent correction or require explicit override based on drift class
